summary: In response to the Linus Tech Tips 'Switching to Linux' challenge series, DeVault offers practical advice for new Linux users while being refreshingly honest about Linux's limitations. He argues that Linux is primarily an operating system built by and for programmers, and that Windows remains the best OS for gaming. Rather than trying to replicate their Windows experience, new users should approach Linux with an open mind and explore what makes it genuinely different. He proposes an alternative set of challenges focused on the shell, package managers, tiling window managers, compiling from source, and community participation.
key_points: Linux is fundamentally shaped by programmers' needs because its open-source nature means those with programming skills can best modify it, making it dominant in technical domains but uneven for mainstream desktop use
Trying to replicate a Windows or macOS experience on Linux is the wrong approach — the value of Linux comes from what it does differently, not from mimicking other platforms
Power users are especially vulnerable to frustration when switching because their deep mastery of one system means essentially starting from zero on another
The LTT challenge tasks (like watching 4K video) fail to showcase what actually makes Linux special and different
Linux's real magic is its collaborative, grassroots community of volunteers supporting each other — something proprietary platforms cannot replicate
money_quotes: The best operating system for gaming is Windows.
Linux users are excited about improved gaming support because it brings gaming to a platform that they already want to use for other reasons.
If you just want to do all of the same things on Linux that you're already doing on Windows, why switch in the first place?
The value of Linux comes from what it can do differently.
Think of it kind of like an app store, but non-commercial and without malware, adware, or spyware.
You don't learn anything by doing the things you already know how to do.
The real magic of Linux comes from the collaborative, grassroots nature of the project, which is something you really cannot get from Windows or Mac.
key_insight: New Linux users succeed not by trying to replicate their Windows experience, but by embracing Linux's fundamentally different strengths — the shell, package management, customization, and community collaboration.
